cue: How do you say you were put on the spot?

es.meaning: Right off the bat; suddenly and without warning, with no time to prepare.
es.example: No puedo decidir de buenas a primeras, déjame pensarlo.
es.exampleTranslation: I can't decide just like that — let me think it over.
es.regionNote: Pan-Hispanic. Mexico also has 'así nomás' and 'de bote pronto'; Spain leans on 'a bote pronto'. Reviewer to confirm which fronts for an MX-default audience.

it.meaning: On the spot; immediately, without time to think it over.
it.example: Non so risponderti su due piedi, fammici pensare.
it.exampleTranslation: I can't answer you on the spot — let me think about it.

bridge.note: One idiom still shows its picture; the other has worn smooth. Italian puts you on your two feet — you are standing, nobody has offered you a chair, answer now. Spanish says de buenas a primeras, a phrase whose literal reading (from goods to firsts) means nothing to anyone, including Spaniards: the image eroded centuries ago and only the function survived. Learners can reason their way into the Italian and simply have to memorise the Spanish.

interference.es_to_it: There is no Italian version of the Spanish phrase to calque — and note the fixed number: su DUE piedi, never 'su un piede'.
interference.it_to_es: Do not reach for feet in Spanish. 'A pie juntillas' exists but means the opposite kind of thing — believing something firmly, not deciding hastily.

review.notes: Discovered via a clip Joe shared 2026-07-20 (discovery only; glosses and examples authored fresh). ES pairing by Claude — UNVERIFIED. Two claims to check: that 'de buenas a primeras' is the closest everyday counterpart for an MX audience, and the a-pie-juntillas contrast in the it_to_es trap.
